summary.what: Hint staged changes after gate abort
summary.why: v0.22.8 closed the phantom-entry hole by refusing timbers log on a dirty tree, but the gate refusal itself still didn't explain WHY the caller's commit had vanished. The unwary agent path was: stage work, git commit, gate aborts, agent runs timbers log expecting it to land — confused state. Surfacing that the staged work is still in the index short-circuits the confusion before the caller reaches the (now-refusing) timbers log call, and the 'inspect with: git diff --cached' phrase points at a one-command diagnostic. Closes timbers-0or.
summary.how: Added internal/git.HasStagedChanges() using 'git diff --cached --name-only' (simpler than parsing --quiet's exit codes; returns false on any git error since this is a diagnostic helper that must not leak failures from the hook). Added the hint line in cmd/timbers/hook_run.go runPreCommitHook, gated on HasStagedChanges() so it stays silent on a clean index. Also reframed 'Run timbers log first' to 'Document the PRIOR commit(s) first: timbers log …' to clarify that the gate is gated by HEAD state, not the current stage attempt. Two new subtests in TestPreCommitHookGating verify fires-when-dirty and silent-when-clean.

notes: Considered making the hint unconditional (simpler) but rejected: a 'staged changes remain' line printed when no staging occurred would mislead a user running 'git commit --amend --no-edit' against an undocumented HEAD. The cost of the conditional is one extra git call per hook invocation, which is well within budget. Considered using 'git diff --cached --quiet' with exit-code parsing; --name-only is clearer and matches the existing HasUncommittedChanges idiom of returning false on error.
